basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Humaza — “The Slanderer” — is an early Meccan sūra that condemns in a single breath two evils closely bound: the slander that wounds, and the wealth that piles up. Tradition reports that it was revealed about a man — al-Akhnas ibn Shurayq, or al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah according to other versions — known for slandering the Prophet to his face and behind his back. But verse 1 uses the word kull (كُلّ), “every”: starting from a case, the condemnation reaches every soul that mocks others believing its possessions shelter it from death. Nine brief verses tracing a relentless movement: from the laugh that scorns, to the count that reassures, to the illusion of lasting forever — and to the Fire that, this one, reaches the very heart.

versets.commentaire: Two words side by side draw a whole moral figure. humaza (هُمَزَة) — from the verb hamaza (هَمَزَ), to prick, to strike — names, according to Mujāhid, the one who wounds with hand or eye: a gesture, a mocking glance. lumaza (لُمَزَة) wounds with the tongue — slander, articulated mockery. The intensive form in -aza says that he has made it a habit. The verse opens with wayl (وَيْل), “woe” — among the strongest interjections of the Quran — and places the moral stress: not on isolated acts, but on a character that has made wounding into a trade.

mots.def: wayl: interjection of threat: woe!; for some commentators, also the name of a valley in Hell.
preposition li- + kull in the genitive: to every, for every.
humaza: intensive form of hāmiz; one who wounds by gesture or look; the habitual fault-finder.
lumaza: intensive form; one who wounds with the tongue, who mocks, who gnaws at others.

versets.trad: who amasses wealth and counts it over,
versets.commentaire: The verse fills in the figure: he gathers — jamaʿa (جَمَعَ) — and he counts — ʿaddada (عَدَّدَ), an intensive form for the repeated gesture of one who runs over his goods, tallies them, recounts them. The Quran here joins two faults rarely set side by side: slander and greed. Why? Because both proceed from the same refusal: refusal of the other, refusal of the gift, refusal of all that exceeds the self.

mots.def: masc. sing. relative pronoun.
jamaʿa: to gather, to amass, to bring together.
māl: wealth, possessions; indefinite accusative.
conjunction: and.
ʿaddada: intensive form of ʿadda (to count); to count and recount with care.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: supposing that his wealth makes him immortal.
versets.commentaire: akhlada (أَخْلَدَ): to make endure forever, to render eternal. The word comes from the same root as khulūd (خُلُود) — the perpetuity that properly is said only of God. The greedy man errs on two counts: he thinks wealth will shelter him from death, and he thinks it binds him to a ground where nothing lasts. A famous ḥadīth says: “If the son of Adam had a valley of gold, he would wish for two.” Greed is not filled — it grows by feeding.

mots.def: ḥasiba: to think, to believe, to suppose; in the imperfect.
subordinating conjunction: that (introduces a clause).
māl + suffix pronoun: his wealth.
akhlada: derived form of khalada (to last forever); to render eternal, to make endure. + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Nay! He shall surely be cast into the Crushing Fire.
versets.commentaire: kallā (كَلَّا): “by no means!”, a sharp rupture that contradicts the illusion. yunbadhu (يُنْبَذُ): to be thrown, to be cast — the verb used for what is discarded as refuse. al-Ḥuṭama (الحُطَمَة), “the Crushing Fire”, is a proper name of Hell here: it comes from ḥaṭama (حَطَمَ), to break, to crush, to shatter. What was amassed shall be crushed; what believed itself to last is cast away.

mots.def: refutation particle: nay, by no means; a strong cut that contradicts what came before.
prefixed affirmative particle: surely, certainly.
nabadha: to throw, to cast, to discard; in the energetic passive imperfect, “he shall surely be cast”.
mots.gram: energetic passive imperfect, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition: into.
al-Ḥuṭama: proper name of the Fire of Hell; intensive form of the verb ḥaṭama (to break, to crush, to shatter).

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what the Crushing Fire is?
versets.commentaire: A Quranic rhetorical question: mā adrāka (مَا أَدْرَاكَ) — what would let you know? — always introduces, in the text, something whose measure exceeds the understanding. Here, what follows is not a full description but an image, suggesting that al-Ḥuṭama cannot be fully named — that it overflows every saying.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
interrogative: what?
adrā: to make know, to inform; perfect + suffix pronoun 2nd pers. masc. sing.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: interrogative: what is.
al-Ḥuṭama: see v. 4.

versets.trad: It is the Fire of God, never extinguished,
versets.commentaire: nāru Llāhi l-mūqada (نَارُ اللَّهِ المُوقَدَة): the Fire of God, ever stoked, never let go out. The passive participle mūqada says that it is ceaselessly kindled, never allowed to die. The Quran does not aim to frighten for frightening: it sets a measure. Faced with what does not go out, the love of what passes — wealth, flattery, the laugh of slander — becomes suddenly slight.

mots.def: nār: fire; in the construct nominative: “the Fire of”.
Allāh: God; in the genitive: “of God”.
mūqada: passive participle of awqada (to kindle, to stoke); ceaselessly stoked, continuously burning.

versets.commentaire: al-afʾida (الأَفْئِدَة): the plural of fuʾād (فُؤَاد), the heart in its most subtle inwardness, the very centre of feeling. The Fire described here does not burn only the skin: it taṭṭaliʿu (تَطَّلِعُ), “rises up to”, “climbs over”, “reaches”, the hearts. This is the essential image: what wounds the other by slander, what hoards for the self alone, one day reaches the very place from which those gestures came — not the outside, but the centre.

mots.def: fem. sing. relative pronoun (referring to al-Ḥuṭama, feminine).
iṭṭalaʿa: to climb up to, to reach the top of; here, the Fire that reaches the innermost of hearts.
preposition: upon, over.
fuʾād: the heart in its subtle centre, the seat of feeling; plural afʾida, the inmost hearts.

versets.commentaire: muʾṣada (مُؤْصَدَة): what is shut, sealed upon. The verse extends the image: not only does the Fire reach the heart, its doors close upon those within. Sūra 90:20 says likewise: “Upon them is a Fire enclosed.” The punishment is not only intense, it is without issue — not that God refuses mercy, but that man, by ceaselessly closing his own heart upon himself, has already closed the door before any other does.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + feminine suffix pronoun: “truly, it”.
preposition ʿalā + plural suffix pronoun: over them.
muʾṣada: passive participle of awṣada; sealed, shut, closed.

versets.commentaire: ʿamad mumaddada (عَمَد مُمَدَّدَة): pillars stretched out, drawn long. Commentators see in them the pillars of the Fire itself — those that hold and seal Hell, or those that stretch out at the horizon like the walls of a closed enclosure. The image, in its obscurity, is deliberate: the sūra closes on something at once vast and shut. And the reader is led back to the first verses: a man who laughs, who counts, who thinks himself immortal. That, in the end, is what the whole sūra was about.

mots.def: preposition: in.
ʿamad: plural of ʿamūd, pillar, column; here, the pillars of the Fire or of Hell.
mumaddada: intensive passive participle of madda; stretched, drawn out, extended in length.
